text: Lecture	  4	  –	  Structure,	  Development	  and	  Growth	   Skeletal	  tissue	  (cartilage	  and	  bone)	   • Made	  up	  of	  cells	  and	  intercellular	  matrix	   • Matrix	   o Fibres	    Collagen	  –	  add	  strength	  to	  CT	    Elastic	  –	  useful	  due	  to	  the	  stretching	  and	  rebound	  abilities.	    Reticular	  –	  support	  individual	  cells	   o Ground	  Substance	  –	  proteoglycans,	  water	  and	  salts.	  Makes	  up	  most	  of	  the	  volume.	  	   	   Fibrous	  Connective	  Tissue	   • Contains	  collagen	  –	  resistive	  to	  stretching.	   • Arranged	  irregularly	  or	  regularly	  (as	  seen	  in	  tendons)	   • Does	  not	  resist	  compression	   	   Cartilage	   • Resists	  compression,	  due	  to	  the	  properties	  of	  ground	  substance.	   • Chondroblasts	  secrete	  extracellular	  matrix	  of	  cartilage.	   o Chondrocytes	  –	  inactive,	  surrounded	  by	  lacunae	  	   • As	  matrix	  increases,	  -­‐blasts	  get	  isolated	  in	  lacunae	  they	  then	  mature	  into	  chondrocytes	  and	  become	   inactive	   • Surround	  by	  perichondrium	  (Dense	  CT),	  this	  contains	  BVs	  that	  supply	  the	  avascular	  cartilage	   	   Types	   • Hyaline	  cartilage	  –	  non	  deformable,	  consists	  of	  collagen	  and	  ground	  substance.	   • White	  Fibrocartilage	  –	  has	  inelastic	  white	  fibrous	  tissue.	  Ratio	  of	  collagen	  and	  ground	  substance	  is	   greater,	  making	  it	  slightly	  deformable	   • Elastic	  cartilage	  –	  Elastic	  fibres	  and	  collagen	   • Articular	  cartilage	  –	  mainly	  collagen.	  Edges	  calcify.	  Found	  in	  joints.	   	   Growth	   • Interstitial	  –	  growth	  by	  chondroblasts	  (forming	  small	  isogenous	  groups)	  from	   within	  the	  cartilage.	   • Apposition	  –	  mesenchymal	  cells	  of	  the	  perichondrium	  become	  chondroblasts	  that	   grow	  more	  collagen.	   o Only	  in	  hyaline	  or	  elastic	  cartilage.	  This	  is	  because	  the	  fibroblasts	  are	   arranged	  in	  rows	  within	  the	  matrix	  (see	  slide	  to	  the	  right),	  so	  there	  would	   not	  be	  sufficient	  cells	  on	  the	  surface	  to	  allow	  for	  appositional	  growth	   	   Bone	   • Has	  calcium	  (calcium	  hydroxyapatite,	  Ca5(PO4)3(OH))	  deposits	  orderly	  distributed	   along	  collagen	  fibres.	   • Can	  resist	  stretching,	  compression	  and	  sheering	  forces.	   • Each	  layer	  is	  dynamic	  with	  osteocytes	  and	  osteoblasts	   • Periosteum	  =	  Dense	  CT	  surrounding,	  	   • Endosteum	  lines	  the	  surface	  facing	  the	  marrow	  cavity	   • Periosteum	  and	  endosteum	  have	  osteogenic	  potency,	  so	  can	  differentiate	   into	  osteoblasts	  to	  repair	  damage	   	   	   	   	   	   	   Histology	  	   • Two	  types:	  Compact/Cortical	  and	  Medullary/Cancellous/Trabeullar	  bone	   • Cortical	   o Arranged	  in	  groups	  called	  osteons/haversian	  units.	   o Each	  osteon	  has	  a	  haversian	  canal	  at	  its	  centre	  that	  carries	  the	  neurovascular	  bundle.	   o Around	  the	  haversian	  canals	  are	  layers	  of	  bone	  called	  lamellae.	    This	  is	  extracellular	  matrix,	  composed	  mainly	  of	  collagen	  fibres,	  laid	  in	  layer	  and	  different	   directions,	  for	  strength	    The	  osteoblasts	  monitor	  and	  regulate	  the	  bone	    The	  osteoblasts	  send	  out	  canaliculi	  that	  connect	  to	  other	  osteoblasts	  &	  haversian	  canals	   • Allows	  communication	  and	  diffusion	  of	  substances	   o From	  the	  Haversian	  canal	  run	  Volkmann’s	  canals.	    These	  carry	  the	  neurovascular	  bundle	  transversely	   • Trabecular	   o Also	  form	  lamella,	  arranged	  in	  all	  manners	  of	  directions.	   o New	  bone	  is	  laid	  down	  according	  to	  the	  needs	  of	  the	  bone’s	  rigidity	   o Has	  spaces	  filled	  by	  marrow	  –	  red	  (Haematopoietic)	  and	  yellow	  (fat).	   • Osteoclasts	  (from	  Haematopoietic	  stem	  cells),	  stimulated	  by	  PTH,	  breakdown	  the	  bone	  matrix	  	   o Located	  at	  the	  edges	  of	  bone.	   	   	   Gross	  Form	  	   • Compact	  bone	  	   o No	  spaces	  in	  the	  bone	   o Usually	  forms	  the	  wall	  of	  the	  diaphysis	  –	  weight	  bearing.	   • Trabecular/Cancellous/Spongy	  bone	   o Bone	  full	  of	  holes	  –	  primary	  centre	  of	  hematopoiesis	  in	  adults.	   • Medullary	  cavity	  that	  produces	  blood	  cells.	   o Infant:	  All	  throughout	  bone	   o Adult:	  only	  in	  heads	   	   Development	   • Mesodermal	  origin	  by	  multipotent	  cells	   • Only	  appositional	  growth	  	   	   Intramembranous	   • Mesoderm	  forms	  membrane	  in	  location	  of	  bone.	   • Form	  ossification	  centre,	  cells	  differentiate	  directly	  into	  osteoblasts	  (produce	  matrix	  which	  ossifies)	   • Eg	  top	  and	  sides	  of	  skull	  (flat	  bones)	  soft	  tissue	  seen	  in	  fontanelles	   replaced	  by	  bone	  joined	  with	  sutures.	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   Endochondral	  Ossification	   • Bone	  begins	  as	  a	  cartilage	  model	  and	  is	  then	  replaced	   by	  bone.	   • Mesodermal	  cells	  differentiate	  into	  chondroblasts	   • Chondroblasts	  produce	  a	  cartilaginous	  bone	  model	   ©Avinash Bharadwaj 2011 32 • At	  the	  centre	  a	  periosteal	  bud	  forms,	  osteoprogenitor	  cells	  invade	  and	  mature	  into	  osteoblasts.	   o These	  start	  producing	  bone	  by	  laying	  down	  calcium	  crystals.	   o Primary	  ossification	  centre	   • The	  bone	  grows	  into	  a	  shaft,	  called	  diaphysis.	   o Secondary	  ossification	  centres	  may	  form,	  usually	  in	  the	  heads	  of	  bone.	   o These	  are	  called	  epiphyses.	   • They	  grow	  towards	  each	  other,	  along	  the	  epiphysial	  plate,	  until	  after	  puberty	  when	  they	  fuse.	   o Chondrocytes	  proliferate	  at	  the	  epiphysial	  plate,	  laying	  down	  cartilage	  (interstitial	  growth)	  	    Thus	  the	  bone	  grows	  in	  length	   o At	  either	  end,	  the	  presence	  of	  calcium	  matrix	  kills	  the	  chondrocytes.	   o Osteoblasts	  then	  use	  the	  calcified	  cartilage	  matrix	  as	  a	  framework	  in	  which	  to	  lay	  bone	  matrix.	   o This	  continues	  until	  a	  bit	  after	  puberty	  finishes	  and	  the	  chondroblasts	  stop	  proliferating	   o The	  epiphysial	  plate	  then	  fuses	  (epiphyseal	  union)	  =	  Synostosis	   • Bone	  is	  dynamic	  and	  living,	  it	  changes	  in	   response	  to	  forces	  placed	  on	  it.	   o Repair	  and	  remodelling.	   	   Primary	  ossification	  centre	  –	  appears	  before	  birth	  	   Secondary	  ossification	  centre	  –	  appears	  after	  birth	   	   	   	   ©Avinash Bharadwaj 2011 33 Growth mechanism
